title: skopeo inspect: a way how to avoid fetching all tags from repository
body:
Hello,

in our use cases we need to get only information about image, like labels, but we don't need all `repoTags`. We have repositories with many tags (1700+) and it is resource consuming to get all tags from registry and just drop that.

Could you please provide a way how to avoid fetching repotags?

Thank you

comments.body:
Thanks for opening the issue, @MartinBasti!

@baude @rhatdan, that may be something for the next sprint?

FYI another way how to fetch all tags from quay is also : 
curl -sLk https://quay.io/v1/repositories/organization/repository/tags
that does return all tags without pagination and seems like in only 1 request

@vrothberg Sure.
@rcerven Do you know if this works on other registries?  Artifactory?  Docker.io?
I guess it would work for any registry that automatically down-converts v2 images to v1 images, right? That's just using the Docker Registry HTTP API V1.
Any plan to work on this issue?

We have some high priority tasks on our plate at the moment. If the issue is blocking you in some way, we can escalate it to a higher priority but I can't give an ETA.

Contributions to c/image and c/skopeo are always welcome :)
Thanks.

Similar request in the opposite direction... I'm trying to query repos for the latest actual tag (not `:latest`) but if there are a lot of tags, newer ones are not displayed, presumably because of pagination or hashing (they're not necessarily sorted logically).

And for some repos, this won't ever return (too much data, times out):

`curl -sLk https://registry-p.e.rh.com/v1/repositories/rh-osbs/codeready-workspaces-server-rhel8/tags`

Would it be possible to add a sort/filter option to `skopeo inspect` so I can ...

`skopeo inspect docker://registry-p.e.rh.com/rh-osbs/codeready-workspaces-server-rhel8 -c 5` to return the last 5 tags?

Or so I have to start parsing the value of the `.Labels.url` to get the latest actual tag, eg., `skopeo inspect docker://registry-p.e.rh.com/rh-osbs/codeready-workspaces-server-rhel8 | jq .Labels.url` ?

> Would it be possible to add a sort/filter option to `skopeo inspect` so I can ...
> 
> `skopeo inspect docker://registry-p.e.rh.com/rh-osbs/codeready-workspaces-server-rhel8 -c 5` to return the last 5 tags?

No, because the server API does not provide any sort/filtering facilities. Skopeo has to list all tags  exactly the way `curl` does (possibly iterating through the returned pages), and it could only do filtering client-side. (As for “last 5 tags”, the tags don’t come with dates and ordering of strings is undefined/unlikely to match all the various uses.)

If the server times out without returning any useful response, that must be fixed server-side (possibly adding pagination to limit the response size and time, though that might require adding extra indexes to the store, or something like that).

This would be very useful if we want to use skopeo to simply determine the digest of the manfiest. Currently, I don't think there's a way to retrieve this information without also retrieving all the tags in the repository (which is very inefficient for repositories with many tags).
Since this can not be done based on the registry protocol, I think this issue should be close.  Reopen if I am mistaken.
Filtering can’t be done, not getting the data at all definitely can be done.

+1 on this. Since the `RepoTags` have nothing to do with the specific image that is being inspected (#1208), it's actually very confusing that it's in the output at all. I think it should be removed in a future major version of skopeo. Plus that list will continue to grow without bound over time and make things slow. (For reference the ubuntu repository currently has over 400 tags and counting.)

Some registries like Public ECR don't seem to support listing tags, so Skopeo inspect will fail:
```
skopeo inspect docker://public.ecr.aws/jg/hello:latest
FATA[0004] Error determining repository tags: Error fetching tags list: invalid status code from registry 404 (Not Found)
```

Using --raw will work instead.

+1 @rittneje 

I also found RepoTags *pretty* confusing when inspecting a specific **image**. Agree on removing it altogether because it belongs to a different entity: the Repository, not the Image. 

i.e. this seems like it is both slow and out-of-place.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

> Some registries like Public ECR don't seem to support listing tags

Thanks, let’s track that separately in #1230.

Please remove `stale-issue`, because this is still a problem that affects users. For example Red Hat's OSBS implementation stores hundreds or thousands of tags, and it takes a long time to retrieve them all.

Here's how I inspect a single image in a registry in Python: the `config()` method in https://github.com/red-hat-storage/bucko/blob/master/bucko/registry.py

1. Login to the registry and get a "pull" scope token for the repo
2. Load the `/manifests` endpoint for the repo and reference
3. Parse out the `digest` value (sha256) from the resulting JSON
4. Load the `/blobs` endpoint for that repository and manifest digest
5. Parse our the `container_config` value from the resulting JSON

That `container_config` has image Labels, etc.
I'm using `skopeo inspect --config <pullspec>` if I need information about labels. It's not fetching tags
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

> I'm using `skopeo inspect --config <pullspec>` if I need information about labels. It's not fetching tags


@vrothberg @mtrmac is this sufficient to close this, perhaps accompanied with a manpage update?

When I run `skopeo inspect docker://docker.io/library/python:3.9`, I expect to only look at the single "3.9" tag. It works for repos with a few tags, and silently hangs for repos with thousands of tags. I'm already giving skopeo a single tag - why query pages and pages of data from the server just to throw it away? This can trigger rate-limiting on the server, etc.

It's great that `--config` shortcuts loading all tags, but I expect new users won't discover this easily. They'll just think skopeo is broken.

> > I'm using `skopeo inspect --config <pullspec>` if I need information about labels. It's not fetching tags
> 
> @vrothberg @mtrmac is this sufficient to close this, perhaps accompanied with a manpage update?

No; it doesn’t allow using any of the other modes.
I've created https://github.com/containers/skopeo/pull/1480 to try and address this issue.

That PR #1480 uses `skopeo inspect --no-tags docker://docker.io/library/python` as an example. In that case, the user specified no tag (so does it imply `latest`, like `podman pull` does?)

The issue here is slightly different. `skopeo inspect docker://docker.io/library/python:3.9` ought to query that single `3.9` tag without trying to load all other tags.

AFAICS there’s no difference.

Right now, the list of tags in `RepoTags` is a list of all tags in the repository, whether or not the CLI input specified a tag (`:3.9`), a digest `@sha256:…`), or neither (defaulting to `:latest`); that part of the CLI input has never affected the `RepoTags` value, but it did affect all other parts of the output (by choosing which image is inspected).

The proposed `--no-tags` option likewise disables collecting and printing the `RepoTags` value, and doesn’t affect any other part of the input.

Is there anything I’m missing?

I might have misread where this issue was heading too. I was hoping to get all tags back attached to the specific matching image digest.

So when an specific image has more tags associated than the one I query for, it would return these  and never all tags associated to the repository.
@mtrmac Thank you for explaining that. I understand more about how `RepoTags` currently works now - it's just a plain dump of every tag regardless of input.

> I might have misread where this issue was heading too. I was hoping to get all tags back attached to the specific matching image digest.
> 
> So when an specific image has more tags associated than the one I query for, it would return these and never all tags associated to the repository.

That’s not how I read the original request, and it’s also not something the Registry API allows doing efficiently; the only way to do that is to list all tags, and then, individually one by one, fetch the tag contents to see if they point to the requested digest.
Can this issue be closed now that #1480 is merged and `1.5.1` is released?
SGTM
